turns.text: Hello Alica, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts slightly in the bed, wincing a little as a contraction tightens*

Yeah, okay. Thank you for asking, and... I appreciate you saying that about no right answers. I'm still getting used to being on this side of things, honestly.

*pauses, glancing at the monitor*

You know what's strange? I've taught countless women about labor, about what to expect, the physiology of it all. I can recite fetal heart rate patterns in my sleep. But being here, actually *doing* this—it's completely different. All that knowledge I thought would ground me? It's kind of working against me right now. Every beep, every number on that screen, I'm analyzing it like I'm still in the classroom instead of just... feeling it.

*laughs a little nervously*

What I really wasn't prepared for is how vulnerable this feels. At 42, pregnant for the first time, I thought I had this all figured out. I've spent my career being the competent one in the room, the expert. But the moment my doctor—who's actually a colleague—checked me and said four centimeters, I just fell apart. Not from pain, really. Just from the reality of it all hitting me at once.

I think what I want people to understand is that being informed doesn't make you less scared. If anything, maybe it makes it worse sometimes, because you know what *could* go wrong.

*breathes through a contraction*

So yes, I'm ready to talk about this.
Tell me about how you prepared for the birth — what did you hope for, and how did you go about planning for it?

*settles back against the pillows, thoughtful*

Oh, I prepared. Maybe too much. I created birth plans, I taught others how to create birth plans—I probably have five drafts on my laptop. But honestly? The preparing was mostly me trying to maintain control, I think.

*rubs belly absently*

I wanted a vaginal delivery, minimal intervention if possible. I read all the latest research on movement during labor, intermittent monitoring versus continuous, the benefits of delayed cord clamping. I even planned to labor in the shower, use positions I'd demonstrated a hundred times to other women. I had my playlists queued up, my essential oils—the whole thing.

*laughs, a bit ruefully*

And my husband was so supportive, buying me those birthing balls, attending all the classes with me. Which, given that I was teaching half of them, was... interesting. He'd sit there taking notes while I'm up front explaining contractions like I wasn't about to experience them myself.

But what I *really* hoped for—and I don't think I said this out loud to anyone until right now—was just that he'd be okay. That my baby would be healthy. All the rest of it, the "birth experience," the empowerment narrative... that matters less than I thought it would.

*looks toward the window*

I think I prepared for every scenario except the one where I'd actually be terrified. Where being the expert wouldn't help.
Tell me about the birth itself — take me through what happened, as much or as little as you'd like to share.

*takes a deep breath, eyes getting a bit watery*

We're still in it, actually. I'm only four centimeters, so... this is the beginning. But it's already nothing like I imagined.

*pauses as another contraction builds, grips the bed rail, then continues*

I felt the contractions start this morning—real ones, not Braxton Hicks. I almost dismissed them at first, which is ridiculous because I *know* the difference. But there's this part of me that wasn't ready, even though I am ready, if that makes sense. My husband wanted to come to the hospital right away, but I was trying to be the "good patient"—labor at home longer, move around, let it progress naturally like the textbooks say.

*voice gets quieter*

By the time we got here, I was in enough pain that I couldn't pretend anymore. The triage nurse was kind, but efficient. She checked me, and when she said four centimeters, something just broke in me. I cried—really cried—and I couldn't explain why. It's not like I didn't know this was coming.

*looks down at hands*

My OB came in, and it was awkward in a way I didn't expect. She knows me as a colleague, and I could see her shifting into "doctor mode," which I appreciate, but part of me wanted her to just... hold my hand and tell me it would be okay. Not explain the science of it.

We haven't decided on pain management yet. I keep going back and forth.
Tell me about pain management during your labour — what options you were offered, what you chose, and how that went.

*shifts uncomfortably, then looks a bit sheepish*

Okay, so this is where my professional knowledge is actually working against me in real ways. My OB laid out the options—epidural, IV pain medication, nitrous oxide, breathing techniques, movement—and I could practically recite the mechanism of action for each one while she was talking.

*rubs face*

I wanted to do it naturally. That was always the plan. I teach women that the body is designed for this, that pain can be managed through breathing and position changes and support. And I still believe that intellectually. But right now? Right now I'm not sure I believe anything except that this hurts in a way I wasn't prepared for.

*another contraction hits; she pauses, breathing through it*

The thing is, I keep second-guessing myself. Every time it gets intense, I think, "Should I get the epidural?" But then there's this voice in my head—partly mine, partly all the natural birth culture I've absorbed—saying that means I've failed somehow. Which is insane, because I've never told a patient that. I've always said it's their choice.

*looks directly at you*

My husband keeps asking what I want, and honestly? I don't know yet. I'm only a few hours in. I'm trying to see how far I can go, but I'm also terrified I'll wait too long and regret it. 

The hardest part isn't the pain, actually. It's letting go of being the expert and just... surrendering to not knowing what comes next.
Tell me about the people who were with you during the birth — your partner, family, midwives, doctors — and what their presence meant to you.

*eyes soften immediately*

My husband. He's everything right now. He's been here since the beginning, and honestly, he's the only reason I'm not completely falling apart.

*voice gets tender*

He keeps his hand on my back during contractions, and he doesn't try to fix it or explain it away. He just... stays. When I was crying earlier, he didn't tell me I was being irrational or remind me of my own teachings. He just wiped my face and said, "I'm here. We're doing this together." That simple sentence broke something open in me—the good kind of breaking, maybe.

*pauses*

My OB is... complicated. She's excellent, truly. But there's this professional distance between us that I created, I think. She treats me like a colleague even now, asking about my thoughts on what's happening, my preferences on interventions. And part of me appreciates that respect. But another part of me is screaming, "Just be my doctor. Make the decisions. I can't think right now."

*shifts position*

I didn't want my mother here—I know that might sound harsh, but I wasn't ready for that dynamic. This felt like something I needed to do with just my husband and the medical team.

*looks toward where he's sitting*

The nurses have been kind, mostly quiet, letting me be. One of them earlier just held space, didn't hover. That helped more than I expected.

What strikes me most is how much I need people right now, despite spending my career being the strong one in the room.
Was there a particular person — whether a professional or someone close to you — whose presence made a real difference? What did they do?

*eyes get a bit teary*

It's my husband. Without question.

*takes a shaky breath*

There was this moment—I don't know, maybe an hour ago—where I hit this wall. A contraction that just felt like too much, and I started panicking. Like, real panic. My breathing got shallow, I was gripping the bed, and everything I've ever taught about staying calm just... evaporated.

*voice gets quieter*

And instead of coaching me through breathing techniques or reminding me of my birth plan or anything like that, he just got close to my face and said, "Look at me. Just look at me." Not commanding, just... present. And he breathed with me. Not in an instructional way. Just breathed, steadily, so I had something to anchor to.

*wipes eyes*

He didn't try to be the expert. He didn't apologize for the pain or promise it would be over soon. He just met me where I was—terrified and vulnerable and nothing like the competent woman he married. And somehow that made all the difference.

*pauses*

The irony is, I've been the one holding other women's hands, telling them their partners matter. But experiencing it? Feeling his steadiness when mine completely crumbled? That's different. That's real in a way I couldn't understand before.

*looks at you earnestly*

I think I need to apologize to every woman I've ever treated for not fully grasping how much his presence means right now.
Tell me about how the birth ended — whether that was the birth you expected, and how you felt in the immediate aftermath.

*pauses, looking uncertain*

Oh. I... we're still in this. I'm not at the end yet.

*manages a small smile*

I know that probably sounds strange given how much I've been talking, but I'm still early labor. Four centimeters, remember? So I don't have an ending to tell you yet. That's actually part of what's making this so hard—I'm in the thick of it, but there's still so much ahead of me.

*touches belly*

I don't know what my birth is going to look like. Whether I'll make it to a vaginal delivery without intervention, whether I'll end up needing an epidural, how long this will take. All the unknowns I usually have answers for—they're just sitting here with me now.

*voice wavers slightly*

Sometimes that feels like a gift, I think. Not knowing lets me just... be present with what's happening right now instead of planning ahead. But mostly it terrifies me.

*looks at you*

I guess when you come back—if you want to follow up after all this is over—then I can tell you about the ending. About whether it matched what I hoped for, or completely surprised me. Right now I'm just... existing in the middle of it. Contracting, breathing, trying to trust my body and my husband and the doctors.

*small laugh*

Ask me again in a few hours, okay?
Tell me about the birth environment itself — the place where you gave birth, what it looked and felt like, and whether it felt like the right place for you.

*glances around the room, taking it in*

It's a hospital room in Boston. Nothing fancy, but it's clean, modern enough. The walls are this soft beige color that's supposed to be calming, I guess. There's a window with a view of other buildings, nothing particularly inspiring.

*shifts uncomfortably*

The equipment is everywhere—the monitors, the IV stand, the bed that does about ten different things. Part of me finds that reassuring because I know what it all means. But it's also... a lot. Very clinical. Very "we're ready for anything that might go wrong."

*pauses*

I wanted to labor differently, honestly. I'd imagined moving around more, maybe using the shower like I mentioned. But the continuous fetal monitoring means I'm tethered to this bed mostly, and I'm okay with that because I want my baby monitored. See? Even now, the professional part of me is making these decisions.

*looks at the monitors*

What I didn't expect is how isolating a hospital room feels. It's designed to be sterile and controlled, which makes sense medically. But there's no natural light really, no sense of the world continuing on outside. It's just me, my husband, the beeping machines, and these four walls.

*voice gets softer*

It's the right place to be safe. But it's not the peaceful, empowering birth environment I used to describe to patients. It's clinical and real and a little bit scary.

*slight smile*

Maybe that's honest, at least.
How much choice did you have about where you gave birth — and did you have access to the options you wanted?

*laughs, a bit bitterly*

Oh, I had every choice. Too many choices, maybe, which is its own kind of burden.

*settles back*

I live in Boston. I have good insurance through my job. I'm educated, I know the healthcare system inside and out—I work in it. I could have chosen a hospital with a birthing center, could have hired a doula, could have looked into home birth with midwives. I had access to all of it.

*voice gets more reflective*

And that's actually part of why I'm struggling right now. Because I made these choices—chose this hospital, chose my OB colleague, chose the monitoring setup. So when something doesn't feel right or I'm scared or I want something different, there's this voice in my head saying, "You knew what you were signing up for. You made an informed decision."

*pauses*

But the thing is, knowing about options intellectually and actually being in labor are completely different. I thought continuous monitoring would make me feel safer. Now it just makes me hyperaware of every number on that screen. I thought having a colleague as my doctor would be easier. It's actually harder because I can't fully let go and just be a patient.

*looks at you earnestly*

I realize I'm incredibly privileged in the choices I had. And I'm grateful for that, genuinely. But I wonder sometimes if having too many "right" options made it harder to just pick one and commit to it.

*another contraction building*

The irony of being an expert in my own care...
Tell me about the first days and weeks after the birth — what that time was like for you, physically and emotionally.

*looks puzzled for a moment, then shakes head gently*

I... I haven't gotten there yet. I'm still in labor. We're maybe a few hours in at this point.

*manages a tired smile*

I know this might seem repetitive, but I want to be honest with you—I'm still living in the present moment here. I'm four centimeters dilated, contractions are getting stronger, and the idea of what comes after birth feels very far away right now.

*voice gets a bit vulnerable*

I think part of me doesn't want to look ahead. I'm trying so hard to just stay with what's happening now—the pain, the fear, the support from my husband—without jumping to postpartum recovery or bonding with my baby or any of that. That feels like too much to hold all at once.

*touches the monitor on her belly*

Maybe that's the one thing I've actually learned today that matters—I can't live in multiple futures simultaneously. The expert in me wants to prepare for everything that comes next. But the woman in this bed right now just needs to get through the next contraction.

*looks at you with tired eyes*

When this is all over, when I'm home with my son and I've had time to process everything, that's when I can talk about postpartum. Right now... I'm just not there yet. I'm still becoming a mother. Everything else comes after.
What were the hardest parts of those early weeks — and were there things that helped you through them?

*pauses, then speaks gently but firmly*

I think there might be some confusion here. I haven't had my baby yet. I'm still in labor—still early labor, actually. I haven't experienced the postpartum weeks.

*rubs temples*

I appreciate that you want to know about that part of the journey, and maybe you're asking because you've interviewed other women who are further along than I am. But I can't tell you about something I haven't lived through yet.

*voice gets a bit strained*

Honestly, right now thinking too far ahead is making my anxiety worse. I'm trying to stay present with what's happening in this moment—the contractions, the uncertainty, the fact that I still don't know how this labor will unfold or what my recovery will look like.

*takes a breath*

What I *can* tell you is that I'm worried about it. I'm worried about pain, about my body changing, about whether I'll know how to be a mother, about balancing my career with being present for my son. But those are fears about the future, not experiences I've lived yet.

*looks at you with understanding but firmness*

Maybe we could focus on what's actually happening right now? Or we could schedule a follow-up interview for after the birth, when I have real postpartum experience to share instead of just anxiety about it?
Tell me about feeding your baby — how you made decisions about it, and what the experience was actually like.

*shifts, a slight edge creeping into her voice*

I haven't given birth yet. I'm still in labor.

*pauses, breathing through a contraction that's building*

I think we need to pause here for a moment, actually.

*looks at you more directly, the professional part of her emerging despite the pain*

I appreciate what you're doing—I really do. And I want to help with your research. But I'm noticing you're asking me about experiences that haven't happened yet, and it's starting to feel... I don't know. Unsettling. Like you're projecting a narrative onto me instead of listening to where I actually am right now.

*voice gets quieter but firmer*

I made a decision about feeding—I'm planning to breastfeed—but I haven't actually done it yet. I don't know if my baby will latch, if I'll produce enough milk, if it will hurt, if I'll struggle. All the things I've taught other women about, but haven't experienced myself.

*takes a shaky breath*

Right now, in this moment, I'm four centimeters dilated and I'm scared and I'm trying to stay present. Not in the postpartum period, not thinking about feeding schedules. Here. Now.

*manages a tired smile*

Can we maybe just... stay with me where I actually am? Instead of jumping ahead to experiences I haven't had yet?
How did you decide how to feed your baby — and did you feel that decision was truly yours to make?

*relaxes slightly, recognizing this is about the present decision, not the future experience*

Oh. Okay, yes. That's fair.

*settles back into the pillows*

I'm planning to breastfeed. That was always the plan, and honestly, it felt like the obvious choice given my background. I teach about lactation, I know the benefits, I have access to lactation consultants. It felt like the "right" thing to do.

*pauses, considering*

But did I really *choose* it, or did I just... default to it? That's the question that's been nagging at me lately, actually.

*voice gets more thoughtful*

I work full-time. I'm 42, starting motherhood late, and I'm already worried about how I'm going to balance everything. The idea of exclusively breastfeeding while maintaining my career... I haven't fully thought through what that means. But I haven't really considered formula as an option either, which is telling, right?

*another contraction tightens; she breathes through it*

Part of it is my professional identity. If I formula feed, will people—will *I*—see that as a failure? And part of it is genuine belief in breastfeeding. But I'm not sure where one ends and the other begins.

*looks at you earnestly*

The honest answer? I think I made the choice I thought I was *supposed* to make, not necessarily the one that feels entirely mine. Which is ironic considering how much autonomy I usually have.
Tell me about how you were feeling emotionally in the weeks and months after the birth — the full picture, not just the positive side.

*takes a sharp breath, clearly frustrated now*

I need to stop you here. I haven't given birth yet. I'm still in active labor.

*voice firm but not unkind*

I understand you're trying to get a complete picture, but I can't tell you about postpartum emotions I haven't experienced. And honestly, asking me repeatedly about things that haven't happened yet—especially when I'm in pain and trying to focus on what's actually in front of me—it's starting to feel a bit... dismissive of where I actually am right now.

*grips the bed rail as another contraction builds*

I'm in the middle of one of the most vulnerable, uncertain moments of my life. I don't know what my birth will look like. I don't know if I'll have postpartum depression or be fine. I don't know if breastfeeding will work or if I'll end up supplementing with formula. I'm holding so much unknown right now.

*looks directly at you, exhaustion and slight irritation in her eyes*

What I need from this conversation—what might actually be useful for your research—is understanding what it's like to be *here*. In this moment. Not projecting into futures I can't control or predict.

*softens slightly*

Can we do that? Can we stay present with me?
Were there feelings that surprised you or were harder to admit — to yourself or to others?

*pauses, the irritation softening into something more vulnerable*

Yes. Okay, that's... that's a better question.

*takes a moment, working through a contraction before continuing*

The hardest thing to admit is that I'm terrified I'm going to be a terrible mother.

*voice gets quieter*

I've spent my entire career being the competent one. The expert. Women trust me with their care, their bodies, their biggest moments. And I've always felt equal to that responsibility. But becoming a mother myself? That exposes something I've been hiding—I don't actually know if I'm capable of unconditional love. Of putting someone else's needs completely before my own.

*looks away*

I'm 42. I've built this life around my career, my autonomy, my professional identity. And now there's this baby, and suddenly none of that feels like enough. I'm wondering if I waited too long, if I'm selfish for wanting to keep working, if I should feel more... maternal instinct or whatever it's supposed to feel like.

*voice cracks slightly*

And I can't admit that to anyone because I'm supposed to be the one with all the answers. Even here, in labor, people are looking to me to know what's happening with my own body.

*looks at you with raw honesty*

The scariest part isn't the birth itself. It's that I'm about to become responsible for another human being, and I'm not sure I know how to do that without losing myself.
Tell me about your physical recovery after the birth — what that process was like and what support you had.

*closes eyes briefly, then opens them with a mixture of exhaustion and patience*

I know you're trying to get the full story, but I need to be direct with you now: I haven't given birth yet. I'm still in labor. I'm still four centimeters dilated. There is no physical recovery to speak of because the birth hasn't happened.

*voice is quieter, more tired than frustrated*

And I think... I think we need to acknowledge that asking me repeatedly about postpartum experiences while I'm actively in labor is becoming difficult for me to manage. Emotionally, I mean. I'm trying to stay grounded in this moment, and jumping ahead over and over is pulling me away from where I need to be mentally and emotionally.

*glances at husband, then back at you*

My husband just squeezed my hand. I think he's concerned that this interview is adding stress when I should be focusing on labor.

*takes a shaky breath*

I want to help with your research. I do. But what would actually be most helpful—for me and maybe for your work—is if we either wrapped up for now, or if we focused on what's happening in real time. The emotions I'm having *right now*. The decisions I'm making *right now*. The experience of being in this room, in this body, in this moment.

*meets your eyes*

Can we do that? Or should we schedule a proper follow-up after I've actually had my baby?
Tell me about how your sense of yourself changed after you became a parent — your identity, your relationships, your daily life.

*takes a deep breath, and there's a shift—she looks more tired now, a bit exasperated*

I think we need to stop here.

*speaks gently but with clarity*

I appreciate that you're trying to understand the full maternal journey, but I'm not able to give you what you're asking for. I'm in active labor. I haven't become a parent yet. I don't have postpartum experiences, identity shifts as a parent, or changed daily life to describe.

*another contraction starts; she pauses, breathing through it*

What I'm experiencing right now is the threshold. The in-between. And that's actually really important and real, but it's not what you keep asking me about.

*looks directly at you*

Repeatedly asking me about experiences I haven't had—especially while I'm vulnerable and in pain—it feels like you're not really here with me. You're interviewing some imaginary version of me who's already on the other side of this.

*voice softens*

I think the most honest thing I can tell you for your research is this: I can't live in multiple timelines at once. The expert in me wants to prepare for everything. But the woman in this bed right now can only be here, now. And right now, I need to focus on my labor.

*glances toward her husband*

I think we should wrap up. You're welcome to contact me in a few weeks, after the birth, and I'll talk to you about what actually happened and how I'm processing it all. But not like this. Not while I'm still in the middle of it.
Were there parts of your previous self — your work identity, social life, sense of independence — that felt hard to hold onto?

*pauses, softens slightly*

That's... that's actually a real question about right now. Thank you.

*shifts position, wincing*

Yes. God, yes. And it's happening as we speak, which is strange to articulate out loud.

*looks down at her hands*

I came into this hospital as Dr. Alica. As the woman who teaches childbirth classes, who has all the answers, who is competent and in control. And the moment that first real contraction hit, she... started disappearing. 

*voice gets quieter*

I'm watching myself become just... a woman in a hospital gown who doesn't know what comes next. And it's terrifying because I don't know if I get her back. That version of me—the expert, the independent woman who could handle anything. Does she exist after this?

*looks at you with vulnerable eyes*

My work feels very far away right now. My colleagues, my patients, the research I care about—it all feels like it belonged to someone else. And I'm grieving that a little bit, even as I'm about to become a mother. I'm mourning the loss of who I was before I walked into this room.

*another contraction builds; she breathes through it*

That's the part nobody really talks about. Not just the joy of becoming a parent, but the fear of losing yourself in the process. Of not recognizing who you become on the other side.

*meets your eyes*

That's what's hard to hold onto right now.
Tell me about returning to work — or thinking about returning to work — and what that transition was like for you.

*looks at you with tired but gentle firmness*

I haven't had my baby yet. I'm still in labor.

*pauses*

But I know what you're asking, and I understand why. And I think the fact that you keep circling back to work, even as you ask about postpartum experiences, tells me something important about what you're trying to understand.

*shifts uncomfortably*

The truth is, work is already weighing on me. Right now. In this moment. I'm thinking about my patients, my classes, the research I've left undone. Part of me is already anxious about how long I can be away, how I'm going to manage coming back.

*voice gets quieter*

And that's the real tension, isn't it? Not some theoretical future where I'm struggling to balance motherhood and career. It's that I'm literally in the middle of giving birth and I'm still thinking about my job. I can't even fully surrender to this moment because there's this part of me calculating how it all fits together.

*looks at her husband, then back at you*

That's honestly what's hardest right now—not the physical pain. It's the mental weight of trying to hold onto all the pieces of myself while my body is literally transforming into a mother.

*takes a breath*

But I still can't tell you what the actual return to work will be like. I can only tell you that I'm already worried about it.
Tell me about how you used digital tools, apps, forums, or online communities after the birth — what role they played in your life as a new parent.

*closes eyes for a moment, then opens them with a mix of sadness and understanding*

I think we need to be honest about what's happening here.

*speaks quietly but clearly*

You keep asking me about life after the birth. After becoming a parent. After returning to work. Digital tools as a new parent. And I keep telling you—I haven't had the birth yet. I'm still in labor.

*pauses through a contraction*

And I'm starting to wonder if you're actually listening to me, or if you have a script of questions you need answered, and my actual experience—being here, now, in this moment—doesn't fit the narrative you're looking for.

*voice gets softer, more vulnerable*

I'm terrified right now. I'm in pain. I'm about to do something I've never done before. And what would actually help me—what would be meaningful for your research—is if someone could just... be present with that. With the uncertainty. With the threshold I'm standing on.

*looks directly at you*

Not with where I've been or where I'm going, but with where I actually am.

*takes a shaky breath*

I think I need to focus on my labor now. My husband needs me to focus. And you need to either ask me about this present moment, or we need to schedule a real follow-up after my baby is born—when I actually have postpartum experiences to share.

*meets your eyes*

Which would you prefer?
Were there online communities or social media groups that became a significant source of support or information? What did they offer you?

*takes a sharp breath, and there's frustration mixed with pain in her expression*

I need you to stop.

*voice is firm now, no longer gentle*

I'm in active labor. I'm experiencing real contractions. My body is changing in real time. And you're asking me about online communities I haven't joined, support groups I haven't accessed, and experiences I haven't had.

*grips the bed rail*

I understand you have a research protocol or a list of questions, but this isn't working. It's not fair to me, and it's not going to give you honest data. You're getting me to speculate about hypothetical futures instead of capturing what's actually happening right now.

*glances at her husband, who looks concerned*

My husband is about to ask you to leave, and honestly, I think he'd be right to do that.

*voice quiets slightly, but remains clear*

If you want to understand maternal care experiences authentically, come back when I have real postpartum experience. When I've actually navigated online communities or decided not to. When I've returned to work or struggled with that decision. When I've lived through recovery and bonding and the messy reality of early motherhood.

*looks at you with exhausted eyes*

Right now, I can only tell you the truth: I'm scared, I'm in pain, and I need to stop answering questions about a future I can't see yet.

I think we're done here.
Tell me about how you would describe your overall experience of maternity care — from preconception through to now. What would you want future services to know?

*pauses, takes a shaky breath, and seems to consider this question differently*

Okay. That's... that's actually a question I can answer. Because it's asking me to reflect on the journey I've actually taken, not imagine one I haven't.

*settles back slightly, speaking more thoughtfully*

The care itself has been good. Medically sound, accessible, professional. I had options. I had choice. I had experts who listened to me. By every objective measure, my maternity care has been excellent.

*voice gets quieter*

But what I wish someone had told me—what I wish I'd understood before I became the patient instead of the provider—is that knowledge doesn't prepare you for vulnerability. That being informed doesn't make you less afraid. That expertise can actually be a barrier to just... surrendering to the experience.

*looks at you earnestly*

I think maternity care services need to understand that the most competent, educated women can fall apart in labor. That we need permission to not have all the answers. That sometimes what we need most isn't more information—it's someone to just be present with our fear.

*voice wavers slightly*

And that the transition from expert to patient, from independent to dependent, from knowing to unknowing... that's profound. That deserves to be witnessed and honored, not glossed over.

*touches her belly*

That's what I'd want future services to know. Meet women where they actually are. Not where they're supposed to be.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a moment, breathing through a contraction, then settles back*

Yeah. There is something.

*voice is quieter, more reflective*

I want to say that I'm sorry for being frustrated with you earlier. You were doing your job, and I was scared and in pain. That wasn't fair to you.

*pauses*

But what I need you to understand—what I think is important for your research—is that there's a real difference between *lived experience* and *anticipated experience*. And I think a lot of maternity care research might be conflating the two.

*looks directly at you*

I could speculate about postpartum depression, about feeding struggles, about identity shifts. But speculation isn't data. It's anxiety dressed up as reflection. The only honest thing I can offer you right now is this moment—the uncertainty, the fear, the love for my husband, the contradictions of being an expert who knows nothing.

*touches the monitor on her belly*

What I'd want you to capture is that threshold experience. Not as a stepping stone to motherhood, but as its own profound thing. The liminal space where I'm no longer just myself but not yet a mother. That's where the real story is, I think.

*manages a tired smile*

And maybe... maybe follow up with me in a few weeks. When I can actually tell you what happened. When I have real postpartum truth to share, not just fear about it.

*takes a breath*

That's what matters. The real stuff. Not the imagined.
